sections.summary: Defines the scope: applies to commercial bitter varieties of cassava roots (Manihot esculenta) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging, excluding cassava destined for industrial processing.

Establishes quality provisions consisting of minimum requirements (product condition and handling) and a three‑class classification system (Extra, Class I, Class II) with specified tolerances and allowed defects for each class.

Lists minimum quality requirements for cassava: must be whole, sound (no rot/mould), clean (practically free of visible foreign matter except permitted substances), practically free of pests/damage, free of abnormal external moisture, free of foreign smell/taste, firm, practically free of mechanical damage/bruising, no flesh discolouration, distal cut ≤2 cm and stalk cut 1–2.5 cm where applicable, and mature enough to withstand transport and handling.

Defines three quality classes: 'Extra' (superior, virtually defect‑free), Class I (good quality with limited slight defects—shape, scarring ≤5% surface, scraped areas ≤10%), and Class II (allows greater defects—scarring ≤10%, scraped areas ≤20%) with the condition that defects must not affect the flesh or essential characteristics.

Defines the 'Extra' class for cassava as superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation; footnotes define commonly used names, state that 'bitter' varieties contain >50 mg/kg cyanides (as HCN, fresh weight) and reference the General Standard for Food Additives (CODEX STAN 192-1995).

Class I cassava must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/type, and may show slight shape defects, scarring/healed damage up to 5% of surface area, and scraped areas up to 10% of surface area, provided these do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping quality, presentation or the flesh.

Class II covers cassava that fail higher classes but meet minimum requirements, allowing defects in shape, scarring/healed damage up to 10% of surface area and scraped areas up to 20% of surface area, provided the produce retains essential characteristics for quality, keeping quality and presentation and the flesh is not affected.
Size is determined by diameter at the thickest cross-section with Size A 3.5–7.5 cm, B 7.6–10.0 cm and C >10.0 cm, and in all cases cassava must weigh at least 300 g and be at least 20 cm long.

Allows tolerances by package for produce not meeting the declared class: quality tolerances permit 5% (Extra), 10% (Class I) and 10% (Class II) by number or weight under specified conditions, and size tolerances allow 10% by number or weight of cassava corresponding to the size immediately above and/or below that indicated on the package.

Specifies quality tolerances per class: Extra—5% by number/weight not meeting Extra but meeting Class I; Class I—10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II; Class II—10% not meeting the class nor minimum requirements, except produce rendered unfit by rotting or similar deterioration.
Permits up to 5% by number or weight of cassava in an 'Extra' class package that do not satisfy Extra requirements but meet Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
Permits up to 10% by number or weight of cassava in a Class I package that do not satisfy Class I requirements but meet Class II or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

Permits up to 10% by number or weight of cassava in a Class II package that satisfy neither the class nor the minimum requirements, except those affected by rotting or other deterioration that renders them unfit for consumption.
For all classes, allows 10% by number or weight of cassava in a package to correspond to the size immediately above and/or below the size indicated on the package.

Requires package contents to be uniform in shape and contain only cassava of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size with the visible portion representative of the whole; packaging must protect the produce using new, clean, food‑grade materials and non‑toxic inks/glues and comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 44-1995), and containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Each package must be uniform in shape and contain only cassava of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, and the visible part must represent the entire contents.

Cassava must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean, food‑grade materials (recycled food‑grade allowed) and non‑toxic inks or glues for printed materials, and packaging must comply with CAC/RCP 44-1995; containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Containers must satisfy quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance criteria to ensure suitable handling, shipping and preservation of cassava and must be free of foreign matter and smell.

Labelling requirements include specific provisions for consumer packages (name and type, and mandatory preparation instructions) and for non‑retail containers which must bear legible, indelible particulars such as identification, nature/type, origin, commercial identification (class, size, net weight, preparation instructions) and an optional official inspection mark, with accompanying footnotes on recycled materials, point‑of‑sale information for unpackaged bitter cassava, and code‑mark usage.

In addition to CODEX STAN 1-1985, consumer packages must be labelled with the name of the produce and type (bitter) and must include preparation instructions stating that cassava must not be eaten raw, must be peeled/de‑pithed/cut/rinsed and fully cooked before consumption, and that cooking or rinsing water must not be consumed or reused.
Each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and the type (bitter), and may include the variety name.

Mandates a label statement that cassava must not be eaten raw; must be peeled, de‑pithed, cut into pieces, rinsed and fully cooked before consumption; and that cooking or rinsing water must not be consumed or used for other food preparation.

Non‑retail containers must bear legible, indelible particulars visible from the outside or in accompanying documents, including identification (name/address of exporter/packer/dispatcher or optional code), nature/type (and variety optional), country of origin (and optionally district/place), commercial identification (class, size, net weight, preparation instructions) and an optional official inspection mark, with footnotes on recycled food‑grade materials and point‑of‑sale information for unpackaged bitter cassava.

Requires name and address of exporter/packer/dispatcher on non‑retail containers, allows an optional identification code (with national rules on code‑mark disclosure), notes recycled material may be used if food‑grade, and states that information on safe handling/preparation must be made available at point of sale for unpackaged bitter cassava.
Requires the name of the produce and its type (e.g., "bitter") on the label when the contents are not visible from the outside; the variety name is optional.
Labels must state the country of origin and may optionally include the district where grown or a national, regional or local place name.
Commercial identification on the label must include class, size (size code or minimum and maximum diameter in centimetres), net weight, and preparation instructions (see Section 6.1.2).
Heading only indicating an optional official inspection mark; no substantive requirements are provided in this section.

Produce must comply with the Maximum Levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex pesticide maximum residue limits; in the absence of a Codex maximum level for hydrogen cyanide, an acceptable maximum shall be set on a safety basis by the national legislation of the importing country.

Recommends that produce be prepared and handled according to relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice (e.g., General Principles, Code for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables) and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under the Codex Principles for Microbiological Criteria (CAC/GL 21-1997).
